speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 29th of March, 1995. OK, Gershon, we'd like to finish up by you saying what you did in Israel, in Palestine, after the war, or just the last few months that you recall before the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine, like I said, I worked for this-- on these various jobs. I then decided, having seen life in the kibbutz, that this-- I wasn't sure whether this was going to be the life for me. I'd seen people that had lived there for 15, even 20 years leaving after that time with their-- with their families. I said, OK I'm 18 years of age. I've now lived in the kibbutz life for three years. I think it's time to go out, try a different way of life, and then decide. If I want to go back, well, the door was open to me. I was set. I can come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I then decided, being a mechanic, I bought an old wreck of a panel van. I converted it to a dual personality, if you like, a dual job. Because I found out that I could do-- the two jobs I could get. One was delivering cakes for half a day, and one was delivering dry cleaning. So I had these two facilities in it. And, of course, one person wasn't allowed to know that I was doing the other. The dry cleaner might not have been happy if I had chocolate cake in there in the morning. Where were you based? What town in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lived in Tel  Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not an uncle or--

speaker: interviewee
text: With a family, but I had a room. And I have no money, but I was by myself. But I had family-- I had an uncle there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him? Was that-- You saw him regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And I had another uncle I saw regularly. And-- but I was completely independent from them. And I still also saw the lady-- remember that I told you that was with us, that came with us all the way, the Mrs. Hammerschmidt?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in close contact with her. And we were very-- and remained very close friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Vitus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vitus, after the kibbutz disappeared. I haven't seen him since. I made many efforts. In fact, last-- sorry-- last year we had a reunion, and I was hoping he would be there. Unfortunately, he wasn't. We had a reunion of the people that went, that were in the kibbutz together. And we did meet quite a few of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where was I? I bought this truck, and I did the deliveries. I made a living for myself, and I started to get myself organized and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I then got an offer, actually-- where Mrs. Hammerschmidt had something to do with-- of becoming a member of Egged. Egged was the best cooperative. It's still in existence today. In those days, however, it was one of the elite things. It was-- you had to have money to get in. But, through a number of things-- I was given the opportunity to become a member without money because they wanted me to look after their fleet, and this and that and the other. And I then decided, now is the time, before I start something new, something bigger-- I had in the meantime, of course, written to my father. The war had finished. My father kept on saying, come and visit. And I said, well you are a Zionist. You've been a Zionist all your life. You come and live here. Backwards and forwards, he convinced me, seeing that I was 20 years of age, it's a lot easier for me to travel than it is for him. And so he sent me a ticket. And I said my goodbyes, coming to Australia for a visit to my father, to be reunited with him. So that's where I started my journey to Australia. I took the train to Port Said, joined a ship called the there.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with 16 other boys, who I didn't know before. I joined the ship there. And we went on a four-week journey, as far as Fremantle, where we were suddenly dumped. They needed the ship for troop evacuation from Singapore. And we stayed there for two weeks, put up at a local community.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so you were 21 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we then made our way over to-- to Sydney, which in itself took a week by train. And arrived here-- I think I told you the date-- was the 23rd of September, 1947. It happened to be a Shabbat and was a bit embarrassing for me. Because my father [INAUDIBLE] had walked all the way from Bondi to Central Railway. --and my arriving by train on the Shabbat with two suitcases. In the meantime, of course, he had also remarried. And my stepmother was with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meta. And she-- we took a taxi back. I couldn't carry the suitcase. And my father, the [INAUDIBLE] one, had to walk back to Bondi, which was a bit embarrassing. Anyway, I stayed with them for a while, and started to-- to-- after a few weeks, you know, I took a job. Are you interested in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a brief overview of what you end up doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a job on a spinning mill mainly to learn English. I stayed there for a few months. I then went to work--

speaker: interviewer
text: You continued living with your father and his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I then took a job as a motor mechanic. I worked for a local Sydney company for two years. I then left that, started in partnership with a very good friend of mine, name of Fred Stein. We went up north and we decided that he was sick of his job. I was sort of wanting to get ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided, well, what can we do without money, this and that? We'd go hawking-- hawking, meaning filling a car up with goods and going door to door on farms. We started that. We did that for a couple of weeks, then hit on the idea that we would go to the, then being built, Snowy Mountain scheme.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were the first people allowed into the scheme to set up shop in their canteens. Excuse me. I have to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [DRINKS] we continued doing this for a few months, until we met with an unfortunate traffic accident. And then ran out of money and couldn't replace a vehicle, and this, that, and the other. Well that brought that episode to an end. There was a very interesting episode. We saw a lot of the country of Australia. In the meantime, I had, of course, kept up my religious attendances again. And I used to go to Central Synagogue. This is the old Central Synagogue in Grosvenor Street. There was a very pretty young lady up in the galleries. And we used to sort of accidentally, intentionally meet. And she's a lady that is the reason why I eventually stayed in Australia-- why I didn't go back. We've now been married for 42 years, happily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Betty. And I hope you'll meet her. Basically, that's-- well, that's really not the end of it. After that business affair, I went back to trucking. Ran that for a while, and then decided to sell that enterprise, and was asked to join Betty's father in their traditional business, which is manufacturing of dance wear, ballet shoes, originally, and then clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this after you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you married in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in 1953. And that's a business we've carried on since. We're still in it. Although, by now, more or less retired. And it's being carried on by our two sons-- Simon, who is doing Australia and surrounds, and David, who is doing the international side and is currently living in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say to do with your travels, and your experiences, and your running throughout the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not the person to give lectures to the world. I think I'm amazed when I sometimes see young people in today's age who are complaining about all sorts of things. We're living in a different world. I find it sometimes difficult to comprehend how people can live the way they do. But I realize, at the same time, it's a different generation. For my part, I must say, we've been blessed with children. Of course, Simon's got a twin sister who was for a while in the business. She's got two lovely kids, is married to a very nice guy. And we've tried to instill in them the values of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your years have affected the way you've brought them up? Your years of hardship and traveling on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, maybe in the wrong way. Maybe because we suffered a lot, maybe we try to give them too much. You can turn this anyway you like. All I know is that as far as I'm concerned, we want to-- we've tried, like most other parents, to set an example to our children and to give them the best we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm sure you have. Do you ever dream about what happened to you? Do you have any nightmares or dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are things. There are occasions. I feel that-- I get-- I'm a very emotional person anyhow. And I don't particularly feel that I have to, particularly now as I'm getting older-- or as I am old. I don't know how to--

speaker: interviewer
text: No you're not.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which is a great way to put it. But I-- when we went back to Germany-- to come back two years ago-- two or three years now, we went back to Dessau, we went back to Aken. To come back and see where the synagogue stood, to relive those experiences, to see what has happened to those communities is very difficult to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's one thing. But as a result of-- you know, you go back-- and this is something I meant to say before. And I'm going back a long way, a number of tapes now. When I was in London one weekend-- I just want to say this because I think it's important. I had a weekend free. I decided to go back to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't remember. I had wiped that out of my mind-- I didn't remember the address of that home that we were in. But I really wanted to know what had happened. Because your mind says, OK, we got away. Those girls got away. What happened to the other 40? So I took myself back to Hamburg. And interestingly enough, without knowing where it was, I got on to the loop, the railway. And I remember that I got to St. Paoli. And then I remember two stations later the train goes down. And if I come to a place-- I mean I know it's called the Schlump now. If I go out that certain door-- anyway, I finished up. It just came back to me, the address [NON-ENGLISH], and I knew where it was, it is now-- or it was a few years ago-- a youth hostile again for students.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did find that there is an old people's home next door. I found an old lady there who remembered us being in the home. And she told me that not long after we had gone, that some tarpoline covered trucks turned up. And they took the whole lot away. They just emptied the house out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we weren't too early. You know, we did the right thing in that respect. You did the right thing. What year did you see this lady, did you meet this lady, when you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the-- no, when was it? I can't remember, in the '60s. It's a long time ago. But I had this urge to go back and see maybe I can find somebody. And I'm really pleased I did. Even though the news I got was unfortunate and was depressing. But at least it gave me the satisfaction to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did the right thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, sad as it was, that we did the right thing. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll meet your family. We'll look at your photos and meet your family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gershon, could you tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, as you see on top it's got my name on top so I don't have to explain who it is. This is a photo that was taken of me in Palestine, or in Israel now. I don't know-- you know what I'm talking about. --At the age of 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photograph, Gershon?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my late mother. This was taken in 1938, or around about that time in Aken Elbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in this photograph, Gershon?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my late father, Isidor Wilkenfeld. And that was also taken around '39, '38, '39 year back in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Aken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Gershon, who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this photograph-- it's not quite 100% clear, but it's of a tiny photograph which I had reproduced. It's of my sister, Berta, and my little brother, Nissan. It was taken in 1939, also in Aken. There's a whole series of photographs that were taken at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried some of them with me. It's amazing what-- what I carried with me. And I also got some of these photographs-- my father did the same thing, and he had some. So I've since been collecting all sorts of things from my aunts, from my father, what he left behind and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Gershon, what is that document?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a letter written by my father-- if you can see, 'prisoner of war mail,' for the time that he was interned in Victoria, Australia, to the last known address of my mother, which is here. And you can see the Nazi stamp on here, which says, 'Geoffnet,' or it's been opened by them to see what it contained. And you will also see, there is a stamp here, which is not too eligible, which says [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That means 'to go back.'

speaker: interviewee
text: To go back. And it says, [GERMAN], which means it's impossible to deliver. Written in pencil on top here, it. Says, [GERMAN], which translated into English means concentration camp, Warsaw. And this was written on by the postman. I have a couple of other letters where he actually signed it, Ambrosius, which was the name of the-- and that's why I know that he is the one that wrote that letter. And that is, of course, written when they were taken away. We have a date here, the 21st of May, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that letter was returned to him, to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What are those photographs of?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, here we have two photographs taken in-- during our escapade to Zagreb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read what it says?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Croatia. And we see here, a photo taken in Zagreb in 1941. And we have in it the lady, name of Recha Freier, who was the lady that organized the whole children transport I spoke of before. We have Mrs. Hammerschmidt, who was an old lady that accompanied us, peeping out from behind here. And we have three other people, a lady and these two gentlemen, who were-- they were Jewish partisans, actually. Well they joined the Jewish partisans, but who were looking after us and the people that I talk about that collected us from the streets when danger was there, or that looked after us and made sure we got food and generally were in charge of those children. On this second photo, a group of the children-- or then children-- who were with us. And this was taken already in Belgrade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you In that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in this photo. I'm right there. This is a very close friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I'm still very much in-- in touch with those boys. And this is just a small group of the people, the closest group, and the same boys that also went to [NON-ENGLISH], and stayed together until that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Gershon, what is in that photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this photograph is a photo of the street called Banhoffstrasse, where I spent my youth. And it's the street where we lived in one of these buildings, in number 15. Where I spoke of before, on Kristallnacht that when I rode back on my bike to see what had happened at our place. And I was pleased to see that nothing happened there. So this is just a photograph of the street we lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken many years later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken 50 years later when we went on a visit to look at-- my-- not my birthplace but the place we lived in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewer
text: You all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, these three photographs, they're not easy to explain. But I'd liked to take you back to the Kristallnacht that I explained before. On the first one on the left here is, as I explained, this whole area that I'm pointing out here was a whole square which was a synagogue. This building here is part off the synagogue-- or the only part that remained standing. This was the area where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about Dessau Anhalt. And the-- actual synagogue part was in this area here, with the little courtyard inside. This is where the rabbi used live, and we used to have classes and so forth. The Interesting part is, of course, in the meantime streets have changed. We went back 50 years later. However, to-- the point of interest here was-- you will see here, and you probably will not see it on that paper, there is a monument here, which is only appearing as a very faint line there. And the detail in here is here. And it says-- I must put my glasses on for this-- 'Den Judischen Mannern Frauen und Kindern Die Den Naziterror Von 1933 Bus 1945,' now I'm mixing a little, 'Zum Opfer Fielen.' It means this is in memory of the people that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: --died as a result of the Nazi terror from 1933 to 1939-- to 1946. I'm sorry. And there is also an inscription around the base of it which says, here stood the synagogue of the city of Dessau, which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --built.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] or consecrated-- excuse me, I have to take it out-- on the 18th of the second 1908. And [GERMAN] on 9th November, on the 9th of November, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when it was destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when it was destroyed. And so these are the photos of what is left of the synagogue in Dessau today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Gershon. Gershon, could you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, with pleasure. This is the young lady I was talking about that was sitting up in shul-- Betty, my wife, 42 years and still going. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Behind me is Simon, who is Judy's twin. So we have Simon and Judy, their children, Ari. And where's Dzadzi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. Dzadzi there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Talia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we have Talia. Talia is David's daughter. David, as I mentioned before, who is presently living in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just point to David?

speaker: interviewee
text: And David is here, if you can zoom in on that, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the son-in-law as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a son-in-law, Judy's husband, who is overseas at the moment. And David's wife, Jasmine, of course, she's also in Los Angeles. That is the family as it is at the moment. We're waiting for more to come. Anybody who should see the film in the future, just give us a call.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think I've said my piece before. I don't want to give any messages. The fact that we've been married for 42 years I think says it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure it does. Betty, is there anything you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm just very happy that Gershon was able to tell his story. We always were wanting to put it-- to record it properly. It's been a fascinating experience, even though a harrowing one. But I'm very pleased that it's being told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thank you, Betty. Judy, is there anything else? We know-- we know that you did a project when you were in high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just very proud of what my father has achieved in life, and very happy and very proud of him at what he did at his age. It's a story that we've already said to Larry. And we'll tell Jared at the appropriate age. And we're just very proud of their Zaide-- and it's a remarkable story. And he's a remarkable man.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're right. Simon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I'm actually looking forward to-- do we get to see this? This--

speaker: interviewer
text: You get to see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm looking forward very much to seeing the final video, because I'm sure that it will be informative for all of us. Because Dad always leaves out bits and pieces of many stories. He's not exactly always easy coming forward and telling some stories. It will be interesting to see how it turned out. And It's nice to have us all here on video for the record.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry that the others aren't here.

speaker: interviewer
text: As many as there are, there's plenty here. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talia, do you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm just surprised at the story and like he survived, very well. And he helped his friends a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's important, isn't it? What do you know, Ari?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very brave when he was going out of Israel to Australia because there was bad people that were trying to kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Germany. You're right. And it's good that you know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good boy. Thank you very much. Thank you.
